title: Talca

infobox.value: Andes mountains in Talca. **2º row:** (left) Town square of Talca, (centre-top) Catholic University of the Maule, (centre-down) Ancient Library of the University of Talca, (right) architectural contrasts. **3º row:** Old Intendancy of Maule, statue of Bernardo O'Higgins, BCI Bank. **4º row:** 1 Norte street, St. Augustine Cathedral, and 1 Sur street. **5º row:** Nocturnal cityscape of Talca. | |

(Spanish pronunciation: [[ˈtalka]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) is a [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") and [commune](/wiki/Communes_of_Chile "Communes of Chile") in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") located about 255 km (158 mi) south of [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Chile "Santiago, Chile"), and is the capital of both [Talca Province](/wiki/Talca_Province "Talca Province") and [Maule Region](/wiki/Maule_Region "Maule Region") (7th Region of Chile). As of the 2012 census, the city had a population of 201,142.
in 1742 by [José Antonio Manso de Velasco](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Manso_de_Velasco "José Antonio Manso de Velasco").[[5]](#cite_note-utalca-5)

**Talca** (Spanish pronunciation: [[ˈtalka]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) is a [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") and [commune](/wiki/Communes_of_Chile "Communes of Chile") in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") located about 255 km (158 mi) south of [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Chile "Santiago, Chile"), and is the capital of both [Talca Province](/wiki/Talca_Province "Talca Province") and [Maule Region](/wiki/Maule_Region "Maule Region") (7th Region of Chile). As of the 2012 census, the city had a population of 201,142.

The city is an important economic center, with agricultural (wheat) and manufacturing activities, as well as wine production. It is also the location of the [Universidad de Talca](/wiki/Universidad_de_Talca "Universidad de Talca") and the [Catholic University of Maule](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_Maule "Catholic University of Maule"), among others. The Catholic Church of Talca has held a prominent role in the history of Chile.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Demographics")]

According to the 2002 census of the [National Statistics Institute](/wiki/National_Statistics_Institute_(Chile) "National Statistics Institute (Chile)"), Talca spans an area of 231.5 km2 (89 sq mi) and had, in that year, 201,797 inhabitants. Of these, in 2002, 193,755 (96%) lived in urban areas and 8,042 (4%) in rural areas.[[3]](#cite_note-INE-old-3) The city had about 233,339 inhabitants (111,796 men and 121,543 women), according to the 2015 census. The population grew by 15.63% (31,542 persons) between the 2002 and 2015 censuses.[[4]](#cite_note-INE-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [Founding of Talca](/wiki/Founding_of_Talca "Founding of Talca")

The city was founded in 1692 by [Tomás Marín de Poveda](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Mar%C3%ADn_de_Poveda "Tomás Marín de Poveda") and refounded as **Villa San Agustín de Talca** in 1742 by [José Antonio Manso de Velasco](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Manso_de_Velasco "José Antonio Manso de Velasco").[[5]](#cite_note-utalca-5)

The city played a role in Chile's independence. For example, the siege of Talca took place there on March 4, 1814, and the [Battle of Cancha Rayada](/wiki/Battle_of_Cancha_Rayada_(1814) "Battle of Cancha Rayada (1814)") on March 29 and the [Second battle of Cancha Rayada](/wiki/Battle_of_Cancha_Rayada_(1818) "Battle of Cancha Rayada (1818)"), on March 16. 1818. Also, on February 12, 1818, [Bernardo O'Higgins](/wiki/Bernardo_O%27Higgins "Bernardo O'Higgins") signed the Chilean declaration of independence.[[5]](#cite_note-utalca-5)

[Talca Province](/wiki/Talca_Province "Talca Province") was founded in 1833. This ended the dependency of Colchagua Province. Also, Talca was a focus of insurrection during the revolutions of 1851 and 1859.

Talca was partially destroyed by the [1928 Talca earthquake](/wiki/1928_Talca_earthquake "1928 Talca earthquake") and the [2010 Chile earthquake](/wiki/2010_Chile_earthquake "2010 Chile earthquake"), being rebuilt both times. It sits near the epicenter of the 2010 8.8 magnitude earthquake and suffered severe shaking[[6]](#cite_note-6) causing the collapse of much of the historic town centre.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Administration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Talca_Municipalidad.jpg/250px-Talca_Municipalidad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Talca_Municipalidad.jpg)

Municipal building of Talca

As a commune, Talca is a third-level [administrative division of Chile](/wiki/Administrative_division_of_Chile "Administrative division of Chile") administered by a [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_council "Municipal council"), headed by an [alcalde](/wiki/Alcalde "Alcalde") who is directly elected every four years. The 2008–2012 alcalde is Juan Castro Prieto (Independent Democratic Union, centre-right).[[1]](#cite_note-Official-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Munitel-2)

Within the [electoral divisions of Chile](/wiki/Electoral_divisions_of_Chile "Electoral divisions of Chile"), Talca is represented in the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Chile "Chamber of Deputies of Chile") by Sergio Aguiló ([PS](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_Chile "Socialist Party of Chile")) and Germán Verdugo ([RN](/wiki/National_Renewal_(Chile) "National Renewal (Chile)")) as part of the 37th electoral district, which consists entirely of the Talca commune. The commune is represented in the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Chile "Senate of Chile") by Juan Antonio Coloma Correa ([UDI](/wiki/Independent_Democratic_Union "Independent Democratic Union")) and Andrés Zaldívar Larraín ([PDC](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_Party "Christian Democratic Party")) as part of the 10th senatorial constituency (Maule-North).

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Primary sector

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Primary sector")]

The region’s natural resource base is diverse, but agriculture plays a fundamental role in Talca’s economy. Currently, common crops include vegetables, fruit, and cereals, cultivated in rural sectors of the commune such as Huilquilemu, Lircay, El Oriente, Victoria, Aurora, and Panguilemo, among others. However, it is the vineyards that hold particular importance, as the Maule Valley—whose main city is Talca—is the largest wine-producing area in Chile, accounting for about 45% of the national total.
Livestock farming (mainly pigs and poultry) is also significant, as it supports major industries in the region. Mining, once represented by the El Chivato (gold) deposit, is currently inactive.

### Secondary and tertiary sectors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Secondary and tertiary sectors")]

Talca is the main industrial center of the region and the Central Valley. It hosts industries primarily focused on processing the area’s agricultural resources. Prominent sectors include paper, wood, plastics, and metallurgy industries, as well as canning, milling, food, and beverage production—for example, Calaf—and meat and sausage processing (especially beef, pork, and poultry), with Productos Fernández standing out. The wine industry is also notable.
The arrival of immigrant entrepreneurs (Spanish, Arab, Italian, and German) was crucial in strengthening local manufacturing and trade. Additionally, hydroelectric development in the province of Talca further boosted industrial activity, particularly through the Colbún-Machicura power plant, built in the 1980s.
However, several major earthquakes (1928, 1939, and 1960) and the excessive political and economic centralization of Chile around Santiago have hindered Talca’s industrial growth, especially since the second half of the 20th century.
The city is an important commercial and service hub. Its main commercial artery is 1 Sur Street, where, along with 1 Norte Street, most banks, financial institutions, retail stores, and major department stores are located. Talca also has three shopping centers (malls) located in different areas of the city:

* Plaza Maule: located in the eastern sector, it has undergone several façade changes and expansions. Its name bears no relation to the Santiago-based Mallplaza chain.
* Portal Centro: located in the city center, it also houses a clinic on its upper floors.
* Go Florida: located in the southwestern area, designed as an outlet-type shopping space.

In 2018, the number of registered companies in Talca was 6,724.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The Economic Complexity Index (ECI) for the same year was 2.21, while the economic activities with the highest Revealed Comparative Advantage (RCA) index were Glass Recycling (133.98), Uranium and Thorium Mining (133.98), and Forest Fire Control Services (65.89).

## International Relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International Relations")]

The city of Talca hosts a series of international relations institutions, such as the Regional Unit for International Affairs (URAI) of the [Regional Government of Maule](/w/index.php?title=Regional_Government_of_Maule&action=edit&redlink=1 "Regional Government of Maule (page does not exist)"), which is responsible for analyzing and managing the region’s bilateral and multilateral relations with [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the rest of the world; the Regional Unit for Investment Promotion and Attraction; the Regional Office of the National Migration Service; the Regional Office of the [General Directorate for Export Promotion](/w/index.php?title=General_Directorate_for_Export_Promotion&action=edit&redlink=1 "General Directorate for Export Promotion (page does not exist)") (ProChile); the Department of Migration and International Police of the [Investigations Police](/wiki/Investigations_Police_of_Chile "Investigations Police of Chile"); and the Migrant Office of the Municipality of Talca.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Internationalization in Higher Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Internationalization in Higher Education")]

In the field of international relations and higher education, the main actors in Talca are the Office of International Relations of the [University of Talca](/wiki/University_of_Talca "University of Talca")[[10]](#cite_note-10) and the Office of National and International Relations of the [Catholic University of Maule](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_Maule "Catholic University of Maule").[[11]](#cite_note-11)
Although to a lesser extent, student mobility is also promoted through the International Experience Program of the [Santo Tomás University](/w/index.php?title=Santo_Tom%C3%A1s_University_(Chile)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Santo Tomás University (Chile) (page does not exist)")[[12]](#cite_note-12) and the Office of International Relations of the [Autonomous University](/wiki/Autonomous_University_of_Chile "Autonomous University of Chile"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) as well as by technical training centers such as the San Agustín Technical Training Center (CFT San Agustín).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geography")]

The commune of Talca spans an area of 231.5 km2 (89 sq mi).[[3]](#cite_note-INE-old-3) The city of Talca is located 250 km (155 mi) south of [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Chile "Santiago, Chile"),[[5]](#cite_note-utalca-5) south of the confluence of the rivers [Lircay](/wiki/Lircay_River "Lircay River") and [Claro](/wiki/Claro_River_(Maule) "Claro River (Maule)"), in the [Central Valley](/wiki/Chilean_Central_Valley "Chilean Central Valley"). The city is bisected by the [Pan-American Highway](/wiki/Pan-American_Highway "Pan-American Highway").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

Talca has a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate#Warm-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): [Csb](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate#Warm-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate")) with dry summers and wet winters, though it is hotter in summer and cooler in winter than coastal cities like Valparaíso and Concepción.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talca&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Talca has a public bus system managed by the municipal Department of Transportation.[[1]](#cite_note-Official-1) The routes are tracked through [Moovit](/wiki/Moovit "Moovit").[[16]](#cite_note-Moovit-16)
